title: Conservation of energy — energy is never lost, only spread out
examTip: Energy is never created or destroyed — always transferred. If asked where wasted energy goes, say 'dissipated to the thermal store of the surroundings'.
introTips: Think about a bouncing ball — where does all its energy go?
Energy stores are like bank accounts — the total balance never changes.
The words 'lost' and 'used up' are banned in physics — say 'dissipated' or 'transferred'.
intro: Every time you drop a ball, turn on a light, or burn fuel in a car engine, you're watching energy transform — never disappearing, just moving. The law of conservation of energy is the reason engineers can't build a perpetual motion machine, why your phone battery runs flat, and why insulating your house saves money. Understanding where energy actually goes — not just where we want it to go — is the foundation of all energy physics.

keyPoints.heading: The law of conservation of energy
keyPoints.content: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — it can only be transferred from one store to another. The total energy in a closed system always remains constant. This is one of the most fundamental laws in all of physics. Whenever you think energy has 'disappeared,' it has actually been transferred to the thermal store of the surroundings — we say it has been dissipated. A bouncing ball is a perfect example: as it bounces, gravitational potential energy converts to kinetic energy, then some is transferred to the thermal store of the ball, the floor, and the air (as sound and heat) on every impact. That's why each bounce is a little lower — the total mechanical energy decreases with each bounce, but the total energy (including heat and sound) stays exactly the same.
writeBullets: Conservation of energy — energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred between stores
Closed system — a system where no energy enters or leaves from outside
Energy store — a way of holding energy (kinetic, gravitational potential, thermal, chemical, etc.)
Dissipated — energy spread out into the surroundings, usually as thermal energy
Thermal store — the internal energy held by the particles of a substance

keyTerms.def: Energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred between stores
A system where no energy enters or leaves; total energy stays constant
Energy spread out into the surroundings, usually as thermal energy, where it is difficult to use again

keyPoints.memTip: Think of energy like money — you can move it between accounts but you can never create it from nothing or make it vanish.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say energy is 'used up' — gently correct this every time. Energy is transferred, never destroyed.

keyPoints.heading: Useful and wasted energy
keyPoints.content: In any energy transfer, some energy is usefully transferred (it does what we want) and some is wasted — usually as heat or sound. A light bulb transfers electrical energy into two outputs: useful light energy and wasted thermal energy (the heat you feel near the bulb). A car engine converts chemical energy stored in fuel into useful kinetic energy — but also wastes a large amount as thermal energy in the engine and exhaust, plus some as sound. Wasted energy is not 'lost' — it's just in a less useful form, spread into the surroundings. We can reduce wasted energy in several ways: insulation (reduces heat loss), lubrication (reduces friction between moving parts, so less mechanical energy converts to heat), and streamlining (reduces air resistance, so less kinetic energy is wasted pushing through the air).
writeBullets: Useful energy transfer — energy that goes to the intended output store
Wasted energy — energy transferred to an unwanted store, usually thermal or sound
Lubrication — using oil or grease between surfaces to reduce friction and heat wasted
Insulation — material that slows the rate of thermal energy transfer to the surroundings
Streamlining — shaping an object to reduce air resistance and cut wasted kinetic energy

keyTerms.def: Energy transferred to the intended output store
Energy transferred to an unintended store, usually thermal or sound
Using oil or similar material between surfaces to reduce friction and heat loss

keyPoints.memTip: Useful = what you WANT. Wasted = what you DON'T want. Both together must equal the input.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Sankey diagrams are a great way to visualise this — the width of the arrow represents the amount of energy.

keyPoints.heading: Energy dissipation
keyPoints.content: Dissipation means energy spreading out into the surroundings, almost always as thermal energy. Once energy has dissipated, it becomes extremely difficult to use again — it's spread thinly across billions of particles and can't easily be recaptured. This is why a perpetual motion machine is impossible: even tiny amounts of friction or air resistance will eventually dissipate all the mechanical energy, and you can never get it back. Key examples of dissipation: friction between surfaces (mechanical energy → thermal energy — brakes get hot, hands get warm when rubbed), electrical resistance in wires (electrical energy → thermal energy — this is how a kettle heats water, but also why transmission cables waste energy), and sound absorption by walls (sound energy → thermal energy — walls vibrate and warm very slightly as they absorb sound).
writeBullets: Dissipation — energy spreading out into the surroundings as thermal energy, making it hard to reuse
Friction — a force between surfaces that converts kinetic/mechanical energy into thermal energy
Electrical resistance — property of a material that causes electrical energy to be converted to heat
Perpetual motion — impossible because dissipation always converts some energy to unusable thermal energy
Sound absorption — sound energy transferred to thermal energy as vibrations are damped by a material

keyTerms.def: The spreading of energy into the surroundings as thermal energy, making it hard to use again
A hypothetical machine that runs forever — impossible because friction always dissipates energy
A contact force between surfaces that converts kinetic energy to thermal energy

keyPoints.memTip: Dissipate = spread out and dilute. Like a drop of dye in a swimming pool — impossible to collect back.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Emphasise that dissipated energy obeys conservation — it still exists, just as low-grade heat in the surroundings.

keyPoints.heading: Reducing unwanted energy transfers
keyPoints.content: Engineers and designers work hard to reduce wasted energy transfers in devices and buildings. Lubrication: adding oil between moving parts reduces friction, so less mechanical energy is converted to unwanted thermal energy — used in car engines, bicycle chains, industrial machinery. Thermal insulation: materials that are poor conductors of heat slow down energy transfer from warm objects to cold surroundings — examples include fibre glass loft insulation (traps air, reducing conduction and convection), double glazing (air gap between panes reduces heat conduction), and cavity wall insulation (foam or fibre in the cavity of brick walls). Streamlining: shaping vehicles and objects to be aerodynamic reduces air resistance, so less kinetic energy is wasted pushing against drag — used in sports cars, aircraft, cyclists' helmets. Regenerative braking in electric vehicles: instead of wasting kinetic energy as heat in brake pads, the motor runs backwards and acts as a generator, converting kinetic energy back to electrical energy stored in the battery — a clever way to recover energy that would otherwise be wasted.
writeBullets: Lubrication — oil between moving parts reduces friction and cuts thermal energy waste
Loft insulation — fibre glass traps air, reducing conduction and convection heat loss through the roof
Double glazing — air gap between panes reduces heat conduction through windows
Streamlining — aerodynamic shape reduces drag, lowering kinetic energy wasted on air resistance
Regenerative braking — electric vehicle motor acts as generator during braking, recovering KE as electrical energy
Cavity wall insulation — foam or fibre in wall cavities slows thermal energy transfer to outside

keyTerms.def: A material or design that slows the transfer of thermal energy to the surroundings
System in EVs that converts kinetic energy back to electrical energy during braking instead of wasting it as heat
Shaping an object to reduce air resistance and the kinetic energy lost to drag

keyPoints.memTip: LSRI: Lubrication, Streamlining, Regenerative braking, Insulation — four ways to cut wasted energy.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Regenerative braking is a favourite exam topic because it combines energy stores, efficiency, and real-world application.

tables.title: Energy transfer examples — useful output, wasted output, and how waste is reduced

0.question: A ball is dropped and bounces several times before coming to rest. Which statement best explains where the energy goes?
options: The energy is transferred to the thermal store of the ball, floor and surroundings (dissipated as heat and sound)
The energy is destroyed when the ball stops moving
The energy is stored in the gravitational potential store of the Earth
The energy disappears because no more work is being done
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The total energy is conserved — it's transferred to thermal stores (and some to sound), not destroyed.
0.wrongFeedback: Remember: energy is NEVER destroyed. When the ball stops, all its mechanical energy has been dissipated as thermal energy and sound into the surroundings.

2.question: Which of the following is the best description of 'dissipation'?
options: Energy spreading out into the surroundings as thermal energy, making it hard to use again
Energy being destroyed when a machine stops working
Energy being stored in a chemical store for later use
Energy transferred usefully to the output device
2.correctFeedback: Spot on! Dissipation means energy spreading out as low-grade thermal energy — still exists, just very hard to recapture.
2.wrongFeedback: Dissipation doesn't mean energy is destroyed — it means energy spreads out as thermal energy into the surroundings, where it's very difficult to collect and reuse.

3.question: An electric vehicle uses regenerative braking. Which energy transfer describes what happens when the brakes are applied?
options: Kinetic energy → electrical energy (stored in the battery)
3.correctFeedback: Correct! The motor runs as a generator during braking, converting kinetic energy back into electrical energy stored in the battery.
3.wrongFeedback: In regenerative braking, the vehicle is slowing down (losing KE). The motor runs backwards as a generator, converting that KE into electrical energy — rather than wasting it as heat in the brake pads.

commonMistakes: Saying energy is 'used up' or 'lost' — energy is always transferred, never destroyed. Say 'dissipated to the thermal store of the surroundings'.
Confusing energy 'stores' with energy 'transfers' — stores are where energy sits (kinetic, gravitational PE, thermal); transfers are how it moves (heating, working, radiation).
Thinking wasted energy breaks the conservation law — it doesn't. Wasted energy is still energy; it just ends up in an unintended store.
Forgetting that perpetual motion machines are impossible because all real systems have friction and dissipation.
Describing thermal insulation as 'stopping heat' — it slows the rate of transfer, it doesn't stop it completely.
examTips: In any question about energy 'disappearing,' always state it has been dissipated to the thermal store of the surroundings.
When asked how to reduce wasted energy transfers, give a specific method (lubrication, insulation, streamlining, regenerative braking) and explain the mechanism.
Sankey diagrams must be drawn to scale — the width of arrows represents the amount of energy, and all arrows must add up to the input.
The phrase 'closed system' means no energy enters or leaves — the total energy is always constant.
revisionCardBullets: Conservation of energy: energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transferred between stores
In a closed system: total energy always stays the same
Useful energy: energy transferred to the intended output store
Wasted energy: energy transferred to an unintended store, usually thermal or sound
Dissipation: energy spreading out as thermal energy into the surroundings, hard to reuse
Friction converts kinetic/mechanical energy → thermal energy (heat)
Electrical resistance converts electrical energy → thermal energy
Lubrication reduces friction → less thermal energy wasted
Thermal insulation (loft, double glazing, cavity walls) slows heat loss to surroundings
Streamlining reduces air resistance → less kinetic energy wasted
Regenerative braking: KE → electrical energy (stored in battery) instead of heat in brake pads
Perpetual motion is impossible — friction always dissipates some energy
